# Introduction (The Model)


## Overview of RHESSys

### What RHESSys is 

RHESSys (Regional Hydrologic Ecosystem Simulation System) is a process based model of the interactions among energy, water and carbon, and nutrient cycling in the soil and vegetation of the terrestrial environment. As a process based model, RHESSys uses established mechanistic understanding to simulate vegetation growth, water and  nutrient fluxes and stores at scales from plot to regions. The model is both spatial and dynamic which allows RHESSys to simulate how energy/water/carbon/nutrient stores and fluxes are distributed over space and evolve through time. The model uses meteorologic inputs, such as precipitation, as well topographic, species-related vegetation, soil and land use parameters to create a place-based representation.    

RHESSys is designed as a community-model, and as such, the representation of specific process has evolved over time. Key developments include coupling with a fire spread and fire effects model, implementation of overstory and understory vegetation, added complexity to vegetation water/carbon/nutrient cycling mechanisms, representation of flowpaths that occur at different scales including deep groundwater flowpaths, surface flow redirection by impervious surfaces,  hillslope-scale lateral subsurface redistribution and plot-scale water transfers.  The main code for RHESSys is maintained on Github and tracks the evolution of the model.  

### Capabilities 

-   Examples of what can be done with the model


RHESSys as a virtual laboratory and as a digital twin. As a digital twin, RHESSys can provide a place-based estimation of how water resources, carbon sequestration, nutrient export, flood and fire risk may change under a changing climate or with different land management practices. For example RHESSys has been used to estimate how seasonal streamflow may change with earlier snowmelt for specific Sierra Nevada watersheds in California, and how summer flow flows may change for watersheds in the Spanish Pynerness as abandonned pastures are invaded by shrubs. Because RHESSys can be run over a wide range of scenarios. it can be used to explore interfactions between land management and cliamte. For example RHESSys has been used to estimate the impact of fuel treatments on fire size  across historic and possible future climates.  

As a virtual laboratory, RHESSys can be used to understand how  different mechanisms influence behavior. For example,RHESSYs has been used to show how sharing of water between trees and neighboring open space or forest gaps can significantly influence forest drought vulnerability and forest responses to thinning.  As a virtual laborartory, RHESSys can be used to help us understand the impact not only of what we know but also what we don't know. For example, RHESSys has been used to compare how different assumptions species-specific plant strategies for allocating the carbon they photosynthesis to leaves, stems and roots influence long-term productivity, and carbon sequestration. Similarly RHESSys can be used to explore the sensitivity of water/carbon/nutrient fluxes to uncertainty and variation in key parameters. For example, RHESSys has been used to estimate how uncertainty or unmeasured spatial variation in soil properties or within species ecophysiological properties impact estimation of streamflow, forest productivity and drought response.


### Strengths 

-   Integrated Ecohydological Modeling
-   Spatial distribution of the landscape
-   Feedback loops
-   Fire model

### Limitations 

-   High data demands
-   Can be computationally intensive
-   Maybe any processes that are not well represented? Let users know if the model won't fit their research question

There are some key processes that are not include in RHESSys that may be important for specific applications, These include
- sediment transport and erosion
- vegetation reproduction 
- species change
- the influence of the land-surface to the atmosphere (e.g vegetation fluxes changing local climate)
- other nutrients (such a P) that may be limiting 
- fine-scale fire behavior

### Comparison of RHESSys to other models 


-   what this model is appropriate for
-   what the trade-offs are

## Recommended reading/Publications

RHESSys is an eco-hydrologic process-based model - and has similarities to other land-surface models (LSM), hydrologic and earth system models. 
The following papers provide some additional context into process-based ecohydrologic models in general

Fatichi, S., Pappas, C., & Ivanov, V. Y. (2016). Modeling plant–water interactions: an ecohydrological overview from the cell to the global scale. Wiley Interdisciplinary Reviews: Water, 3(3), 327-368.


To learn more about modeling workflow for coupled eco-hydrologic models see:
Brookfield, A. E., Ajami, H., Carroll, R. W. H., Tague, C., Sullivan, P. L., & Condon, L. E. (2023). Recent advances in integrated hydrologic models: Integration of new domains. Journal of Hydrology, 620, 129515. (particularly the Appendix that provides guidance on Selecting an Integrated Hydrologic Model)

A foundational RHESSys paper is 
Tague, C., Band, L. (2004) RHESSys: Regional Hydro-ecologic simulation system: An object-oriented approach to spatially distributed modeling of carbon, water and nutrient cycling, Earth Interactions 8(19): 1-42.


While the Tague and Band (2004) paper provides information on some core process representation, the model has continued to evolve and many of the process representations and model capabilities have changed. The papers below highlight several key changes. Details of specific process representation and how these have evolved are discussed in relevant sections of this book as needed. Papers on specific applications, such as using the model in urban settings or using the model to track distrubance are provided in appropriate sections in this book


*Fire*
Bart, R.R., Kennedy, M.C., Tague, C.L., McKenzie, D. (2020) Integrating fire effects on vegetation carbon cycling within an ecohydrologic model, Ecological Modelling 416: 108880. doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolmodel.2019.108880

*Carbon Allocation in Plants*
Garcia, E.S., Tague, C.L., Choate, J.S. (2016) Uncertainty in carbon allocation strategy and ecophysiological parameterization influences on carbon and streamflow estimates for two western US forested watersheds, Ecological Modeling 342: 19-33. doi: 10.1016/j.ecolmodel.2016.09.021

Deeper Groundwater Hydrology
Tague, C., Farrell, M., Grant, G., Choate, J., Jefferson A. (2008) Deep groundwater mediates streamflow response to climate warming in the Oregon Cascades, Climatic Change 86: 189-210.

*Soil Nitrogen Cycling *
Chen, X., Tague, C.L., Melack, J.M., Keller, A.A. (2020) Sensitivity of nitrate concentration-discharge patterns to soil nitrate distribution and drainage properties in the vertical dimension, Hydrological Processes doi: 10.1002/hyp.13742

*Forest Mortality*
Tague, C.L., McDowell, N.G., Allen, C.D. (2013) An integrated model of environmental effects on growth, carbohydrate balance, and mortality of Pinus ponderosa forests in the Southern Rocky Mountains, PLoS ONE 8(11): e80286. doi: 10.1371/journal.pone.0080286



## Workflow overview

![**RHESSys Modeling Workflow Overview**](images/ModelingWorkflowOverview-01.jpeg){fig-alt="RHESSys Modeling Workflow Overview" width="1000"}

-   Step 1: Installation - Set up the RHESSys environment
-   Step 2: Data Preparation - Gather and format spatial, climate, and validation data
-   Step 3: Preprocessing - Generate the landscape representation (Worldfile) and routing (Flowtable) input files
-   Step 4: Model Setup and Execution - Define the simulation rules and run RHESSys
-   Step 5: Calibration & Sensitivity Analysis - Condition the model (Initialize/reach equilibrium), refine parameters, validate performance
-   Step 6: Post Processing - wrangle, analyze, and visualize output data

### Key concepts and terminology 

(define frequently used terms)

-   Spatial hierarchy (basin, hillslope, zone, patch, stratum)
-   Input File types (Worldfile, Header, Flowtable, Tecfile, Parameter/Climate files, Output filters)
-   State variables
-   Spatial/Temporal step (i.e., basin daily, patch monthly)
